methodology: single-context, no subagents; baseline = answer w/o reading SKILL.md; with_skill = answer after reading SKILL.md + knowledge/architecture/access-methods.md + per-file docs

evals.prompt: I want to write a custom Index Access Method for PostgreSQL. What's the minimum set of IndexAmRoutine callbacks I must implement, and which ones can I leave NULL? Also, what's the rule about ambeginscan and RelationGetIndexScan?
evals.baseline_answer: For a custom index AM you implement an IndexAmRoutine struct returned by a handler function declared via CREATE ACCESS METHOD ... HANDLER my_handler. You generally need callbacks for build, insert, scan begin/end/rescan, and vacuum. Roughly: ambuild, aminsert, ambulkdelete, amvacuumcleanup, amcostestimate, amoptions, ambeginscan, amrescan, amendscan, and at least one of amgettuple or amgetbitmap. Things like amgettuple can be NULL if you only support bitmap scans (gin does this). ambeginscan needs to return an IndexScanDesc. I believe RelationGetIndexScan in genam.c is a helper that allocates the scan descriptor, and ambeginscan should use it — there may be a convention that you must return exactly what RelationGetIndexScan returns rather than allocating your own, because the framework expects that layout.
evals.with_skill_answer: Mandatory callbacks (planner/executor dereferences them unconditionally): ambuild, ambuildempty (builds init fork for unlogged indexes), aminsert, ambulkdelete, amvacuumcleanup, amcostestimate, amoptions, amvalidate, ambeginscan, amrescan, amendscan. You also need at least one of amgettuple or amgetbitmap (gin is bitmap-only). Optional (may be NULL): aminsertcleanup, amcanreturn (only if you support index-only scans), amgettreeheight, amproperty, ambuildphasename, amadjustmembers, ammarkpos/amrestrpos (NULL is fine if amcanbackward=false), the three parallel-scan hooks amestimate/aminit/amparallelrescan (required iff amcanparallel=true), amtranslatestrategy/amtranslatecmptype. ambeginscan rule: it MUST return the IndexScanDesc that RelationGetIndexScan() gave it — index_endscan in genam.c reaches inside the struct after your amendscan runs (genam.c top comment calls this 'kinda ugly'). You don't free the IndexScanDesc yourself; IndexScanEnd does. Don't forget the ~22 flag fields (amcanorder, amcanunique, amcanmulticol, amoptionalkey, amsearcharray, amsearchnulls, amstorage, amclusterable, ampredlocks, amcanparallel, amcaninclude, etc.) — the planner reads them before any callback is invoked, and getting them wrong causes assertion failures or silent wrong results. Register via CREATE ACCESS METHOD myam TYPE INDEX HANDLER myam_handler; the handler returns a pointer to a statically allocated IndexAmRoutine. Reference: src/test/modules/dummy_index_am/dummy_index_am.c is the minimal stub.
assertions: Lists ambuild, aminsert, ambulkdelete, amvacuumcleanup, amcostestimate, amoptions, ambeginscan, amrescan, amendscan as mandatory
Mentions ambuildempty (for init fork of unlogged indexes) as mandatory
Mentions amvalidate as mandatory
States need at least one of amgettuple or amgetbitmap
States ambeginscan MUST return what RelationGetIndexScan() returned
Mentions optional callbacks like ammarkpos/amrestrpos, parallel-scan hooks (conditional on amcanparallel), amcanreturn
Mentions the static flag fields (amcanorder/amcanunique/etc.) and that planner reads them before any callback
References dummy_index_am or a concrete reference implementation

evals.prompt: How does VACUUM interact with an index AM — which callbacks fire, in what order, and what are the semantic requirements? Is ambulkdelete called once or many times?
evals.baseline_answer: VACUUM calls into the index AM via two callbacks: ambulkdelete and amvacuumcleanup. ambulkdelete is the main pass: VACUUM gives it a callback to identify which heap TIDs are dead, and the AM walks its index removing matching entries. amvacuumcleanup is a final cleanup pass that can return stats and may reclaim empty pages. I think ambulkdelete can be called more than once — VACUUM processes the heap in batches when memory is limited, so it may call ambulkdelete once per batch of dead TIDs. amvacuumcleanup runs once at the end. The AM also needs to handle concurrency — scans can run while vacuum is in progress, so deletion has to be safe.
evals.with_skill_answer: Order: ambulkdelete (possibly many times) → amvacuumcleanup (once). ambulkdelete is the pass that drops index entries whose heap TID matches a vacuum-supplied callback; it can be called multiple times because the heap is vacuumed in batches (when dead-TID accumulator hits its memory limit it flushes by calling each index's ambulkdelete, then resumes). amvacuumcleanup is the final pass: it may return stats and reclaim empty pages. Both return IndexBulkDeleteResult*. The AM must work under concurrent readers — each AM picks its own page-level discipline (btree uses Lehman & Yao right-link-and-restart; gist uses NSN versions). VACUUM-related properties on IndexAmRoutine: amparallelvacuumoptions (bitmask of VACUUM_OPTION_*), amusemaintenanceworkmem. Reference for the two-pass cycle-id pattern is nbtree's BTCycleId. WAL durability: every real AM uses a custom rmgr so replay reproduces a state consistent with the pre-crash heap (Generic WAL is the low-frequency fallback). Index AM contract also includes: idempotent inserts under crash recovery, support for at least one opclass before the AM is useful at all.
assertions: States ambulkdelete may be called multiple times (heap vacuumed in batches)
States amvacuumcleanup runs once at the end
States ambulkdelete uses a vacuum-supplied callback to identify dead heap TIDs
Mentions both return IndexBulkDeleteResult* (or that amvacuumcleanup may return stats)
Mentions concurrency: scans run while vacuum runs, AM picks own locking discipline
References amparallelvacuumoptions bitmask or a concrete vacuum-related flag
Mentions nbtree as the canonical two-pass-with-cycle-id reference

evals.prompt: I want to build a columnar table access method. The TableAmRoutine struct is huge — what's the hardest part of implementing one, and what heap-specific assumptions still leak through the API?
evals.baseline_answer: Table AMs were added in v12 and the struct in tableam.h is large — something like 40+ callbacks covering scans, tuple insert/delete/update/lock, parallel scan setup, index fetch, analyze, sample scans, DDL/storage operations like relation_set_new_filelocator, relation_copy_data, vacuum, size estimation. For a columnar AM the hard parts are MVCC (you have to implement tuple_satisfies_snapshot yourself), TIDs (postgres TIDs are 6-byte (block, offset) and indexes assume this layout — columnar doesn't have a natural block/offset, so you have to fabricate stable TIDs), and the visibility map / FSM / TOAST which are heap-specific machinery the planner sometimes assumes exists (e.g. index-only scans consult the VM directly). HOT is heap-specific too — there's no API surface for it but tuple_update returns a TU_UpdateIndexes value (TU_None / TU_All / TU_Summarizing) that gives non-heap AMs a similar optimization. heapam_handler.c is the only in-tree implementation so it's your reference.
evals.with_skill_answer: The hardest part is TID semantics. ItemPointer is a 6-byte (block, offset) pair baked into the index AM interface, WAL, syscaches, pg_class rowcount estimation, and exposed to SQL as tid/ctid. A non-heap AM must fabricate stable, ~48-bit, monotone-ish TIDs for every row and route index_fetch_tuple and tuple_fetch_row_version against them. This is the single biggest reason most experimental table AMs (zheap, columnar) never reach production. Heap-specific leakage that survives the v12 abstraction: (1) ItemPointer / TID format itself; (2) Visibility Map and FSM — only heap maintains them; index-only scans consult VM_ALL_VISIBLE directly and would always see 'no' for a non-heap AM unless that AM also writes a VM fork; (3) HOT — no API; non-heap equivalent is the TU_UpdateIndexes return value of tuple_update (TU_None / TU_Summarizing / TU_All); (4) VACUUM scheduling — relation_vacuum is the callback but autovacuum decisions are driven by pg_stat_all_tables counters heap produces, so non-heap AMs need to fake them or live with autovacuum never visiting; (5) TOAST — relation_needs_toast_table lets you opt out, but opt-in pulls heap-style out-of-line storage; (6) relation_size(MAIN_FORKNUM) presumes block-oriented storage and is used by BRIN + ANALYZE. Also: almost everything is mandatory — tableamapi.c::GetTableAmRoutine has ~30 Assert(routine->X != NULL) lines; only finish_bulk_insert and the TID-range pair (scan_set_tidrange/scan_getnextslot_tidrange) are truly optional, and even tuple_insert_speculative is mandatory because the parser doesn't yet know which AM the table will use. The slot interface: slot_callbacks(rel) returns a TupleTableSlotOps* — you define your own (e.g. heap uses TTSOpsBufferHeapTuple); raw HeapTuple only appears inside the heap AM. Reference: heapam_handler.c (the only in-tree table AM), and the API docs in tableam.sgml.
assertions: Identifies TID semantics (6-byte ItemPointer / fabricating stable 48-bit TIDs) as the single hardest problem
Mentions Visibility Map and FSM as heap-only machinery
Mentions index-only scans consulting the VM directly as a concrete VM-leakage example
Mentions HOT lacks API surface but tuple_update returns TU_UpdateIndexes (TU_None/TU_All/TU_Summarizing)
Mentions autovacuum scheduling depends on heap-produced statistics
States almost all callbacks are mandatory (Assert list in tableamapi.c / GetTableAmRoutine), with only finish_bulk_insert + TID-range truly optional
Mentions TupleTableSlotOps / slot_callbacks as the tuple-movement interface
References heapam_handler.c as the only in-tree implementation
